subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Landfill dW/dt = (1/25)(W-300) — Tangent Approx, Second Derivative & Separation of Variables
stimulus: At the beginning of 2010, a landfill contained 1400 tons of solid waste. The increasing function $W$ models the total amount of solid waste stored at the landfill. Planners estimate that $W$ will satisfy the differential equation $\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{1}{25}(W - 300)$ for the next 20 years. $W$ is measured in tons, and $t$ is measured in years from the start of 2010.

parts.prompt: Use the line tangent to the graph of $W$ at $t = 0$ to approximate the amount of solid waste that the landfill contains at the end of the first 3 months of 2010 (time $t = \frac{1}{4}$).
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Finds slope at $t=0$: $\frac{dW}{dt}\big|_{t=0} = \frac{1}{25}(W(0)-300) = \frac{1}{25}(1400-300) = \frac{1100}{25} = 44$ tons per year
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Slope at $t=0$ is $44$. Tangent line: $y = 1400 + 44t$. $W(1/4) \approx 1411$ tons.

parts.prompt: Find $\frac{d^2W}{dt^2}$ in terms of $W$. Use $\frac{d^2W}{dt^2}$ to determine whether your answer in part (a) is an underestimate or an overestimate of the amount of solid waste that the landfill contains at time $t = \frac{1}{4}$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct conclusion: since $W \ge 1400 > 300$, we have $\frac{d^2W}{dt^2} = \frac{1}{625}(W-300) > 0$; the graph of $W$ is concave up, so the tangent line lies below the graph, making part (a) an underestimate
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\frac{d^2W}{dt^2} = \frac{1}{625}(W-300) > 0$ for $W \ge 1400$. Graph is concave up; part (a) is an underestimate.

parts.prompt: Find the particular solution $W = W(t)$ to the differential equation $\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{1}{25}(W-300)$ with initial condition $W(0) = 1400$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Uses initial condition: $\ln(1400-300) = 0 + C \Rightarrow C = \ln(1100)$; so $W - 300 = 1100e^{t/25}$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\ln(W-300) = \frac{t}{25} + C$. Using $W(0) = 1400$: $C = \ln(1100)$. $W(t) = 300 + 1100e^{t/25}$. 0/5 if no separation; max 2/5 if no constant.
